title: The Rights of Non-Muslims under Islamic Law
subtitle: Life, property, worship, and legal protection — what classical Islamic law guaranteed non-Muslims, what it denied them, and how modern scholars have rethought the framework
summary: Classical Islamic law guaranteed non-Muslims enforceable protections of life, property, and worship that were unusual in the pre-modern world, while also assigning them a subordinate legal rank with real disabilities. This article documents both sides: the Quranic baseline, the Prophet's covenants and their authenticity debates, the dhimma system as jurists actually described it, the lived history of non-Muslim communities under Muslim rule, and the modern critique together with contemporary Muslim scholars' turn toward equal citizenship.

seo.metaTitle: Rights of Non-Muslims under Islamic Law: Dhimma, Jizya, and the Modern Debate
seo.metaDescription: What Islamic law guaranteed non-Muslims — life, property, worship, legal protection — and what it restricted. The Medina Charter, the Pact of Umar, the jizya, hadith on protected persons, and the modern citizenship debate, documented fairly.

sections.title: What This Article Documents
sections.body:
Few subjects in the comparative study of Islam generate claims as far apart as this one. One strand of popular literature describes Islamic law as a machine of permanent oppression for anyone outside the faith; another describes a golden age of tolerance with hardly a shadow in it. The historical record supports neither picture in full. Classical Islamic law guaranteed non-Muslims specific, enforceable protections — of life, property, worship, and legal recourse — that were unusual in the pre-modern world, and it simultaneously assigned them a subordinate legal rank with genuine disabilities that no modern standard of equal citizenship would accept. Both facts are well documented, and both belong in an honest account.

This article works through the evidence in layers: the Quranic baseline governing conduct toward peaceful non-Muslims; the covenants attributed to the Prophet Muhammad, with their authenticity debates stated plainly; the dhimma system as classical jurists actually described it, including its second-class elements presented at full strength; the textual sources on the sanctity of protected non-Muslim life; the lived history of Jewish, Christian, Zoroastrian, and later Hindu communities under Muslim rule; and finally the modern critique of the whole framework, together with the responses of contemporary Muslim scholars — most of whom now argue that equal citizenship, not the dhimma, is the correct Islamic arrangement for the modern state.

Scripture below is quoted verbatim from the translations named in the notes section. Historical claims that remain contested in scholarship are presented as attributed arguments, not as settled facts.

sections.title: The Quranic Baseline: Justice to Those Who Do Not Fight You
sections.body:
Three Quranic passages form the baseline that classical and modern jurists alike cite when they discuss the treatment of non-Muslims who are not at war with the Muslim community.

The first is Quran 60:8-9, revealed in Medina while hostilities with the Meccan polytheists were ongoing. Verse 8 states that God "does not forbid you from those who do not fight you because of religion and do not expel you from your homes - from being righteous toward them and acting justly toward them," and it closes: "Indeed, Allah loves those who act justly." Verse 9 then limits the prohibition to taking as allies those who did fight and expel. The operative line the passage draws runs through hostile conduct, not through belief. A number of early exegetes debated whether the verse was restricted to a particular group or abrogated by the later verses of Surah At-Tawba; the reading that prevailed in the commentary tradition — recorded by al-Tabari in his Jami' al-Bayan and adopted in Ibn Kathir's commentary — treats it as general and operative toward any non-hostile non-Muslim, and modern scholarship across the spectrum follows that reading. Readers should know the abrogation claim existed; it did not become the mainstream position.

The second is Quran 2:256: "There shall be no compulsion in [acceptance of] the religion." Classical commentators connected the verse's revelation to Medinan families who wished to force children raised in Judaism or Christianity into Islam and were told they could not. Later jurists narrowed or debated its scope in various ways, and the tension between this verse and rules classical states applied in other areas (apostasy, for example) is treated honestly in a separate article in this library. But as a statement about the forced conversion of non-Muslims, its plain sense was affirmed across the legal schools: classical law did not authorize compelling Jews, Christians, or Zoroastrians to accept Islam, and forced mass conversion appears in the historical record as the exception rather than the rule.

The third is Quran 22:40, part of the earliest passage granting permission for armed defense. It reasons that "were it not that Allah checks the people, some by means of others, there would have been demolished monasteries, churches, synagogues, and mosques in which the name of Allah is much mentioned." The verse places four kinds of houses of worship — three of them non-Muslim — in a single protected list, and exegetes read it as making the protection of these buildings one of the stated purposes of legitimate war. Classical jurists cited it when ruling that the churches and synagogues of communities under treaty were not to be demolished.

sections.title: The Prophet's Covenants: Medina, Najran, and St. Catherine
sections.body:
The Constitution of Medina (the Medina Charter) is the document the Prophet is reported to have concluded shortly after arriving in Medina in 622 CE, organizing the city's Muslim clans and its Jewish tribes into a single defensive polity. It is preserved in Ibn Ishaq's eighth-century biography of the Prophet (transmitted through Ibn Hisham) and, by a separate route, in Abu Ubayd al-Qasim ibn Sallam's Kitab al-Amwal. One clause, in a common English rendering, declares that "the Jews of Banu 'Awf shall be considered as one community (Ummat) along with the believers — for the Jews their religion, and for the Muslims theirs." Parallel clauses extend the same terms to the other Jewish clans, establish mutual defense, and refer disputes to God and Muhammad. Historians across the spectrum have treated the document as authentically early — including scholars otherwise deeply skeptical of Islamic source material, such as Patricia Crone, who singled it out as solid early matter — largely because its unpolished form and its modest picture of Muhammad's authority are difficult to explain as later invention. Its precise date and whether it is one document or several remain debated, as W. Montgomery Watt's analysis shows.

Honesty requires adding what came after. The charter did not prevent rupture: the sira literature reports that two of Medina's major Jewish tribes were expelled after episodes the sources describe as breaches of the pact, and that the fighting men of a third, the Banu Qurayza, were executed after the Battle of the Trench. Muslim jurists have traditionally framed these events as treason judgments under the covenant's own terms rather than as religious persecution — noting that other Jewish groups remained in Medina afterward — while some modern historians contest details of the transmitted accounts or the framing itself. Both readings are argued in the scholarly literature.

Better attested than the famous Sinai document is the treaty with the Christians of Najran, recorded in early legal and historical works such as Abu Yusuf's Kitab al-Kharaj: it reports guarantees for Najran's lives, lands, religion, priests, and churches in exchange for a fixed levy of cloth, with the stipulation that no bishop or monk be removed from his office.

The covenant with the monks of St. Catherine's Monastery at Mount Sinai is a different case. The document grants the monks protection, forbids destroying or looting churches, and exempts monks from certain levies. The monastery preserves later copies — tradition holds that Sultan Selim I took the purported original to Istanbul in 1517, leaving a certified copy — and most academic historians date the text in its received form well after the Prophet's lifetime, pointing to anachronisms in its formulae and the absence of early attestation. John Andrew Morrow's 2013 study, The Covenants of the Prophet Muhammad with the Christians of the World, argues that the covenant tradition is substantively authentic; reviewers have valued the compiled material while questioning parts of the argument. The fair statement is that the St. Catherine covenant is a venerated document of uncertain date whose provisions match the protective norms found in better-attested sources — and that it should be cited as such, not as an established prophetic text.

sections.title: The Dhimma System: Protection, Jizya, and the Restrictions
sections.body:
Dhimma means a covenant of protection; ahl al-dhimma, 'the people of the covenant,' were non-Muslims who lived permanently under Muslim rule as protected but legally differentiated persons. Jurists commonly connected the institution to Quran 9:29, which commands fighting certain People of the Scripture until they pay jizya. The verse itself does not contain the inserted phrase 'who fight against the Muslim polity.' Some modern interpreters restrict the command through its historical setting or through other verses governing hostilities, while many classical jurists read it more broadly. The article must distinguish the verse's wording from those competing legal interpretations.

Legal manuals commonly describe protection of life and property and some degree of communal religious practice in exchange for jizya and acceptance of Muslim rule. The scope was not uniform. Rules concerning new or existing churches and synagogues, public ritual, clothing, officeholding, testimony, and exemptions from jizya varied by school, treaty, ruler, period, and locality. Some communities enjoyed substantial autonomy and security; others experienced restrictive enforcement or persecution. These historical differences should not be compressed into a claim that one package of rights was consistently guaranteed everywhere.

Now the other side, without airbrushing. The dhimma was a hierarchy, and its subordinating elements were real. The document known as the Pact of Umar (al-shurut al-'umariyya) — attributed to the caliph Umar ibn al-Khattab but dated by most historians to later generations; A. S. Tritton argued in 1930 that it is a juristic compilation, and Milka Levy-Rubin has traced many of its provisions to Byzantine and Sasanian precedents that crystallized into the received text by roughly the ninth century — imposes conditions that include not building new churches in Muslim cities, not raising voices in worship, wearing distinctive dress (the zunnar belt and, in various eras, colored patches or garments known as ghiyar), not riding horses, and keeping houses lower than Muslims' houses. Enforcement varied enormously: long stretches of laxity were punctuated by episodes of strict application, as when chronicles such as al-Tabari's record the caliph al-Mutawakkil's decrees of 850 CE imposing distinctive dress and other restrictions.

The law books add further disabilities. In most schools a dhimmi's testimony was not accepted against a Muslim in court. The schools split on blood-money: Hanafi jurists held the diya of a dhimmi equal to that of a Muslim, while Maliki jurists set it at half and Shafi'i jurists at a third — a hierarchy written into the compensation tables themselves. A Muslim man could marry a Jewish or Christian woman, but a non-Muslim man could not marry a Muslim woman. Conversion ran one way: entering Islam was welcomed, leaving it was penalized. Whatever protective content the system carried — and it carried a great deal — these were second-class terms, and serious Muslim scholarship today says so plainly rather than explaining them away.

sections.title: The Sanctity of Protected Life in the Hadith and Juristic Literature
sections.body:
The textual tradition treats the life and property of protected non-Muslims as inviolable, and the sanctions attached are severe by the tradition's own measure.

The most cited text is in Sahih al-Bukhari, in the Book of Jizya, narrated by Abdullah ibn Amr: the Prophet said, "Whoever killed a person having a treaty with the Muslims, shall not smell the smell of Paradise though its smell is perceived from a distance of forty years" (Sahih al-Bukhari 3166). The Arabic term is mu'ahid — a non-Muslim under covenant, whether the permanent covenant of the dhimma or a temporary guarantee of safety.

A second text, from Sunan Abi Dawud's Book of Kharaj, extends the protection beyond killing to everyday injustice. A number of the Prophet's Companions reported, transmitting collectively from their fathers, that the Messenger of God said: "Beware, if anyone wrongs a contracting man, or diminishes his right, or forces him to work beyond his capacity, or takes from him anything without his consent, I shall plead for him on the Day of Judgment" (Sunan Abi Dawud 3052). Al-Albani graded the report authentic; because the chain rests on an unnamed group of narrators reporting collectively rather than a single named line — a less standard isnad structure — hadith specialists weigh its independent evidentiary strength differently, though its substance matches the better-attested material above.

The caliph Umar ibn al-Khattab's dying instructions, preserved in Sahih al-Bukhari's long account of his assassination, include this charge to his successor: "I also recommend him concerning Allah's and His Apostle's protectees (i.e. Dhimmis) to fulfill their contracts and to fight for them and not to overburden them with what is beyond their ability" (Sahih al-Bukhari 3700). The instruction is notable for its direction: the ruler owes the dhimmis performance of the contract, including military defense on their behalf.

The system's scope also widened in practice. Sahih al-Bukhari 3156 reports that Umar hesitated to take jizya from Zoroastrians until Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf testified that the Prophet himself had taken it from the Zoroastrians of Hajar in eastern Arabia — the precedent by which Zoroastrians entered the protected category alongside Jews and Christians. Centuries later, Hanafi jurists (whose school governed most of the eastern Islamic world) held that jizya could be accepted from any non-Muslim people apart from Arab polytheists, and Muslim states in Sind and later in the Delhi Sultanate and Mughal Empire extended dhimmi status to Hindus, Buddhists, and others as demonstrable administrative practice — the thirteenth-century Persian Chachnama projects this arrangement back to the eighth-century conquest of Sind. Shafi'i jurists formally restricted the dhimma to scriptuaries and Zoroastrians, so the extension was itself a documented point of juristic disagreement. The jurist Ibn Qayyim al-Jawziyya's fourteenth-century treatise Ahkam Ahl al-Dhimma, the fullest classical statement of the law, assembles the rules on both sides of the ledger: the inviolability of dhimmi life, property, and worship, and the discriminatory conditions discussed in the previous section.

sections.title: Fourteen Centuries in Practice: Persistence, Flourishing, and Persecution
sections.body:
Legal texts describe norms; history shows what happened. The single most telling fact is demographic persistence: Jewish, Christian, Zoroastrian, and Hindu communities survived — and in long stretches flourished — under Muslim rule for centuries, which is difficult to reconcile with a policy of extermination or forced conversion, though fully compatible with pressure, disability, and episodic violence.

On the flourishing side of the ledger: the Church of the East kept its patriarchate in Abbasid Baghdad, and the patriarch Timothy I's courteous theological disputation with the caliph al-Mahdi (circa 781 CE) survives as a document of protected coexistence at the empire's center. The great Jewish academies of Sura and Pumbedita operated in Abbasid Iraq, and the geonim who led them corresponded across the Muslim world. The Cairo Geniza documents — the medieval Jewish community's own papers, studied in S. D. Goitein's A Mediterranean Society — show Jews trading, litigating, and living integrated economic lives across the Islamic Mediterranean. After the expulsion of the Jews from Spain in 1492, the Ottoman state under Bayezid II admitted Sephardi refugees in large numbers, and Ottoman Jewry became one of the world's principal Jewish centers. Christians served as physicians, scribes, and administrators in Muslim courts from Umayyad Damascus to Mughal Delhi; Zoroastrians persisted in Iran (though in long demographic decline); and after roughly five centuries of Muslim rule, the Indian subcontinent remained majority Hindu.

On the persecution side, stated at full strength: the Fatimid caliph al-Hakim ordered the destruction of the Church of the Holy Sepulchre in Jerusalem in 1009 along with a wider campaign against churches and synagogues; the Jewish community of Granada was massacred in 1066 in a riot that killed the Jewish vizier Joseph ibn Naghrela; the Almohads in twelfth-century North Africa and Spain abolished dhimmi protections outright and imposed forced conversions — Maimonides' family fled that persecution; al-Mutawakkil's ninth-century decrees humiliated dhimmis by dress and demolished newer churches; and the Ottoman devshirme levied Christian boys for conversion and palace service, a practice Ottoman historians still debate but which no account can call voluntary. Episodes of mob violence, discriminatory enforcement, and confiscation recur across the centuries at particular times and places.

The historian Mark R. Cohen, whose Under Crescent and Cross is the standard comparative study, describes the historiography as trapped between a "myth" of interfaith utopia and a "countermyth" of relentless persecution, and finds the documented reality between them: a legally subordinated but genuinely protected existence, substantially more secure in the medieval period than the Jewish experience in Latin Christendom, and substantially short of equality. Bernard Lewis's The Jews of Islam reaches a similar summary judgment: dhimmi status was second-class, but it was a real, law-governed status — discrimination was structural, while persecution in the sense of violent repression was episodic rather than constant. Presenting either half without the other misrepresents the record.

sections.title: The Critique of the Dhimma and the Modern Muslim Responses
sections.body:
The critique deserves its strongest form. First, hierarchy itself: whatever protections the dhimma carried, it defined rights by religion, and a system in which one community's testimony, blood-money, marriage options, and public religious life are legally restricted is discriminatory by definition — a point made not only by Western critics but by Muslim reformers. Second, the humiliation strand: Quran 9:29's phrase wa-hum saghirun and certain juristic elaborations of jizya collection were read by some classical writers as requiring demeaning treatment, and some eras practiced it. Third, the polemical extension: the writer Bat Ye'or coined the term "dhimmitude" to argue that Islamic civilization is constitutively built on the degradation of non-Muslims. Mainstream academic historians — Cohen most directly, who answered what he called the neo-lachrymose reading of Jewish history under Islam — have criticized that framework as selective and polemical while acknowledging the discriminatory realities it draws on. Readers will meet both the serious critique and the polemical version, and the difference matters.

The classical and modern responses, also at full strength. On 9:29, mainstream exegesis and modern scholarship situate the verse in the campaign context of its revelation (the Tabuk period and the confrontation with Byzantium), and jurists never read it as a warrant for unlimited war on all non-Muslims — the same jurists built the dhimma, treaty law, and the aman (safe conduct) on the assumption of stable peace with non-Muslim peoples. On wa-hum saghirun, an influential line of jurists glossed the phrase as submission to the law of the polity rather than ritual humiliation, and jurists including the Shafi'i authority al-Nawawi rejected degrading collection rituals as baseless additions. On the jizya itself, defenders note it functioned as the fiscal counterpart of the military service and zakat obligations borne by Muslims, was waived for those who served, and was graded and widely exempted — though critics rightly answer that a tax marking religious difference is still a tax marking religious difference.

The most important development is internal. The Ottoman reforms of 1839-1856 (culminating in the Hatt-i Humayun) abolished the jizya and proclaimed legal equality regardless of religion. In 1990 the Egyptian writer Fahmi Huwaidi published Muwatinun la Dhimmiyyun ("Citizens, Not Dhimmis"), arguing that the dhimma was a historically conditioned administrative arrangement, not immutable creed, and that full citizenship is the valid modern Islamic position; Yusuf al-Qaradawi's Non-Muslims in the Islamic Society defended the classical system's protective intent while endorsing citizenship language; and Abdullahi Ahmed An-Na'im's Islam and the Secular State pressed the argument furthest, contending that religious neutrality of the state is the precondition for Muslims and non-Muslims alike to live their convictions freely. In January 2016, hundreds of Muslim scholars convened by Shaykh Abdallah Bin Bayyah issued the Marrakesh Declaration, which invokes the Medina Charter as precedent and calls for developing a jurisprudence of citizenship that includes religious minorities as equals. When the self-styled Islamic State demanded jizya from Christians in Raqqa in 2014, Muslim scholars worldwide — including the signatories of the Open Letter to al-Baghdadi — condemned the act as a violation of both classical conditions and the modern citizenship consensus. The direction of contemporary mainstream Muslim scholarship is not restoration of the dhimma but its supersession by equal citizenship, argued on Islamic grounds.

sections.title: A Comparative Note: Protected Outsiders in Biblical Law and Christendom
sections.body:
The dhimma is best understood inside the family of pre-modern arrangements for religious outsiders, and both biblical law and Christian imperial law offer the natural comparisons — comparisons historians themselves make, not a device for excusing anyone.

Israelite law created a protected category for the resident alien (ger). Leviticus commands: "If a stranger lives as a foreigner with you in your land, you shall not do him wrong. The stranger who lives as a foreigner with you shall be to you as the native-born among you, and you shall love him as yourself; for you lived as foreigners in the land of Egypt" (Leviticus 19:33-34). Exodus states the same rule negatively: "You shall not wrong an alien or oppress him, for you were aliens in the land of Egypt" (Exodus 22:21). The ger was protected and owed love, yet remained legally distinct from the Israelite — a structure of protected difference recognizably parallel to, and far older than, the dhimma.

Christian imperial and canon law likewise governed religious outsiders by restriction: the Theodosian and Justinianic codes barred Jews from public office, from building new synagogues, and from testimony against Christians in various circumstances, and canon 68 of the Fourth Lateran Council (1215) required Jews and Muslims in Christian lands to wear distinguishing dress — the direct Latin counterpart of the ghiyar. Mark R. Cohen's systematic comparison concludes that both civilizations subordinated the religious other, while finding that the medieval Jewish position under Islam was, on the whole, more secure in law and life than under Latin Christendom — a judgment he documents at length and other historians have debated at the margins.

The point of the comparison is calibration, not exculpation. Judged against its own era, the dhimma was a comparatively stable and law-governed form of religious pluralism. Judged against modern equality, it fails — as does every pre-modern arrangement, biblical and Christian included. That is precisely why the contemporary Muslim scholarly conversation summarized above has moved from defending the dhimma to superseding it.

sections.title: Method, Sources, and What Remains Contested
sections.body:
Scripture in this article is quoted verbatim: Quranic verses from the Tanzil Uthmani text with Saheeh International's translation (via api.alquran.cloud), and biblical verses from the World English Bible (via bible-api.com). Hadith are quoted from the standard English renderings used by sunnah.com's numbering: Sahih al-Bukhari 3166 (Book of Jizya), 3156, and 3700, and Sunan Abi Dawud 3052 — the last graded authentic by al-Albani, though its isnad's less standard collective-narrator structure is noted rather than suppressed.

Items presented here as contested, because they are: the date and unity of the Constitution of Medina (authentically early per most historians, but debated in detail); the authenticity of the St. Catherine covenant (defended by John Andrew Morrow, dated later by most academic historians); the attribution and dating of the Pact of Umar (a later juristic compilation per A. S. Tritton, with Milka Levy-Rubin tracing its provisions to Byzantine-Sasanian precedent); the meaning of wa-hum saghirun in Quran 9:29 (submission to law versus humiliation — both readings attested in classical commentary); the legal framing of the Banu Qurayza episode; and the overall balance-sheet judgments of historians such as Cohen and Lewis, which are summarized with attribution.

No casualty figures or population statistics are asserted in this article's own voice. Events such as the destruction of the Holy Sepulchre (1009), the Granada massacre (1066), the Almohad persecutions, and al-Mutawakkil's decrees are reported as the chronicles and standard histories record them; readers pursuing the details should consult the works in Further Reading, which represent the mainstream scholarly literature rather than polemic from either direction.

quranVerses.arabic: لَّا يَنْهَىٰكُمُ ٱللَّهُ عَنِ ٱلَّذِينَ لَمْ يُقَٰتِلُوكُمْ فِى ٱلدِّينِ وَلَمْ يُخْرِجُوكُم مِّن دِيَٰرِكُمْ أَن تَبَرُّوهُمْ وَتُقْسِطُوٓا۟ إِلَيْهِمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُقْسِطِينَ
quranVerses.translation: Allah does not forbid you from those who do not fight you because of religion and do not expel you from your homes - from being righteous toward them and acting justly toward them. Indeed, Allah loves those who act justly.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: إِنَّمَا يَنْهَىٰكُمُ ٱللَّهُ عَنِ ٱلَّذِينَ قَٰتَلُوكُمْ فِى ٱلدِّينِ وَأَخْرَجُوكُم مِّن دِيَٰرِكُمْ وَظَٰهَرُوا۟ عَلَىٰٓ إِخْرَاجِكُمْ أَن تَوَلَّوْهُمْ ۚ وَمَن يَتَوَلَّهُمْ فَأُو۟لَٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلظَّٰلِمُونَ
quranVerses.translation: Allah only forbids you from those who fight you because of religion and expel you from your homes and aid in your expulsion - [forbids] that you make allies of them. And whoever makes allies of them, then it is those who are the wrongdoers.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: لَآ إِكْرَاهَ فِى ٱلدِّينِ ۖ قَد تَّبَيَّنَ ٱلرُّشْدُ مِنَ ٱلْغَىِّ ۚ فَمَن يَكْفُرْ بِٱلطَّٰغُوتِ وَيُؤْمِنۢ بِٱللَّهِ فَقَدِ ٱسْتَمْسَكَ بِٱلْعُرْوَةِ ٱلْوُثْقَىٰ لَا ٱنفِصَامَ لَهَا ۗ وَٱللَّهُ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ
quranVerses.translation: There shall be no compulsion in [acceptance of] the religion. The right course has become clear from the wrong. So whoever disbelieves in Taghut and believes in Allah has grasped the most trustworthy handhold with no break in it. And Allah is Hearing and Knowing.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: ٱلَّذِينَ أُخْرِجُوا۟ مِن دِيَٰرِهِم بِغَيْرِ حَقٍّ إِلَّآ أَن يَقُولُوا۟ رَبُّنَا ٱللَّهُ ۗ وَلَوْلَا دَفْعُ ٱللَّهِ ٱلنَّاسَ بَعْضَهُم بِبَعْضٍۢ لَّهُدِّمَتْ صَوَٰمِعُ وَبِيَعٌۭ وَصَلَوَٰتٌۭ وَمَسَٰجِدُ يُذْكَرُ فِيهَا ٱسْمُ ٱللَّهِ كَثِيرًۭا ۗ وَلَيَنصُرَنَّ ٱللَّهُ مَن يَنصُرُهُۥٓ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَقَوِىٌّ عَزِيزٌ
quranVerses.translation: [They are] those who have been evicted from their homes without right - only because they say, "Our Lord is Allah." And were it not that Allah checks the people, some by means of others, there would have been demolished monasteries, churches, synagogues, and mosques in which the name of Allah is much mentioned. And Allah will surely support those who support Him. Indeed, Allah is Powerful and Exalted in Might.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: قَٰتِلُوا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱللَّهِ وَلَا بِٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْءَاخِرِ وَلَا يُحَرِّمُونَ مَا حَرَّمَ ٱللَّهُ وَرَسُولُهُۥ وَلَا يَدِينُونَ دِينَ ٱلْحَقِّ مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَٰبَ حَتَّىٰ يُعْطُوا۟ ٱلْجِزْيَةَ عَن يَدٍۢ وَهُمْ صَٰغِرُونَ
quranVerses.translation: Fight those who do not believe in Allah or in the Last Day and who do not consider unlawful what Allah and His Messenger have made unlawful and who do not adopt the religion of truth from those who were given the Scripture - [fight] until they give the jizyah willingly while they are humbled.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

bibleVerses.text: “‘If a stranger lives as a foreigner with you in your land, you shall not do him wrong. The stranger who lives as a foreigner with you shall be to you as the native-born among you, and you shall love him as yourself; for you lived as foreigners in the land of Egypt. I am Yahweh your God.
“You shall not wrong an alien or oppress him, for you were aliens in the land of Egypt.

faq.question: What exactly was the jizya, and who had to pay it?
faq.answer: The jizya was an annual poll tax imposed on protected non-Muslim subjects. Classical rules commonly focused on free adult men with means, while exemptions for women, children, poverty, disability, age, clergy, or military service differed across schools and administrations. In return the state claimed duties of protection, and dhimmis ordinarily did not pay zakat as Muslims did. The system nevertheless marked a legally subordinate status based partly on religious identity, and its actual burdens varied considerably across time and place.

faq.question: Were non-Muslims equal to Muslims under classical Islamic law?
faq.answer: No. They were protected legal persons — their lives, property, worship, and communal courts were guaranteed, and killing or wronging them was severely condemned in the hadith literature — but they were not equals. Their testimony was generally not accepted against Muslims, most schools set their blood-money below a Muslim's, they could not marry Muslim women, new church construction was restricted, and some eras imposed distinctive dress. An honest description is protected subordination: real rights, real disabilities.

faq.question: Was the Pact of Umar really written by the caliph Umar?
faq.answer: Most historians think not, at least not in its received form. A. S. Tritton argued in 1930 that the document is a later juristic compilation, and Milka Levy-Rubin has shown that many of its provisions descend from Byzantine and Sasanian precedents, crystallizing into the standard text by roughly the ninth century. It nonetheless became legally influential, and some of its restrictions were enforced at particular times and places — so its late date does not make the restrictions themselves fictional.

faq.question: Did non-monotheists such as Hindus and Buddhists receive dhimma protection?
faq.answer: In practice, yes, across most of the eastern Islamic world. The precedent of taking jizya from Zoroastrians (Sahih al-Bukhari 3156) widened the category beyond Jews and Christians, and Hanafi jurists accepted the dhimma for essentially all non-Muslim peoples outside pagan Arabia. Muslim states in Sind, the Delhi Sultanate, and the Mughal Empire administered Hindus and Buddhists as protected communities for centuries — the subcontinent remained majority Hindu throughout. Shafi'i jurists formally limited the dhimma to scriptuaries and Zoroastrians, so the extension was a documented point of juristic disagreement.

faq.question: Does any country apply the dhimma system today?
faq.answer: No modern state applies the classical dhimma. The Ottoman reforms of 1839-1856 abolished the jizya and proclaimed legal equality, and modern Muslim-majority states are organized around citizenship, whatever their practical shortcomings. Mainstream contemporary scholarship — Fahmi Huwaidi's Citizens, Not Dhimmis, Yusuf al-Qaradawi's work on non-Muslims, and the 2016 Marrakesh Declaration convened by Shaykh Abdallah Bin Bayyah — argues on Islamic grounds for equal citizenship. When the self-styled Islamic State demanded jizya from Christians in 2014, Muslim scholars worldwide condemned it, including the signatories of the Open Letter to al-Baghdadi.

faq.question: Is criticism of the dhimma system just anti-Islamic polemic?
faq.answer: No — and treating it that way would be unfair in the other direction. Serious historians document the system's discriminatory elements, and Muslim reformers themselves have criticized the dhimma as a historically conditioned arrangement unsuited to the modern world. What scholars such as Mark R. Cohen do reject is the polemical extension — the claim, associated with the term 'dhimmitude,' that Islamic civilization is constitutively built on degradation — because the documented record shows protected, often flourishing non-Muslim communities alongside the disabilities and the episodic persecutions. The critique and the polemic are different things.

furtherReading.title: Under Crescent and Cross: The Jews in the Middle Ages
furtherReading.author: Mark R. Cohen
furtherReading.note: The standard comparative study of Jewish life under medieval Islam and Christendom; explicitly answers both the 'interfaith utopia' myth and the 'perpetual persecution' countermyth. Princeton University Press, 1994.

furtherReading.title: Tolerance and Coercion in Islam: Interfaith Relations in the Muslim Tradition
furtherReading.author: Yohanan Friedmann
furtherReading.note: A rigorous study of how classical jurists actually reasoned about non-Muslims, apostasy, and religious hierarchy, built on close reading of the legal sources. Cambridge University Press, 2003.

furtherReading.title: Non-Muslims in the Early Islamic Empire: From Surrender to Coexistence
furtherReading.author: Milka Levy-Rubin
furtherReading.note: Traces the surrender agreements and the Pact of Umar's provisions to Byzantine and Sasanian precedents, showing how the dhimma rules crystallized historically. Cambridge University Press, 2011.

furtherReading.title: Religious Pluralism and Islamic Law: Dhimmis and Others in the Empire of Law
furtherReading.author: Anver M. Emon
furtherReading.note: A legal-theoretical analysis arguing the dhimma rules are best understood as governance of diversity within an imperial legal order — neither simple tolerance nor simple persecution. Oxford University Press, 2012.
